meta.title: R
meta.description: R programming for data science, statistical analysis, and visualization

items.text: Does the code follow tidyverse style conventions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Follow the tidyverse style guide: snake_case for variables, pipe operator for chains, consistent spacing and indentation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent style makes code readable and maintainable. The tidyverse style guide is the most widely adopted R convention.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for camelCase or dot.case naming. Verify pipes are used for multi-step transformations. Run lintr or styler.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses myVar naming. R convention is my_var (snake_case). Run styler::style_file() to auto-format.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow tidyverse style. Use lintr for linting and styler for auto-formatting.

items.text: Are operations vectorized instead of using loops?

baseContent.whatItMeans: R is optimized for vectorized operations. Use apply/map/mutate instead of for loops. Use dplyr verbs for data frame operations.
baseContent.whyItMatters: For loops in R are orders of magnitude slower than vectorized operations. A loop over 1M rows takes seconds; vectorized takes milliseconds.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for for loops iterating over data frame rows. These should usually be vectorized with mutate(), apply(), or map().
baseContent.exampleComment: This for loop calculates a new column row by row. Use mutate(df, new_col = old_col * 2) for a 100x speedup.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Vectorize operations. Use dplyr, purrr, and apply family instead of explicit loops.

items.text: Are repeated operations extracted into well-documented functions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Extract repeated code into functions with clear names, roxygen2 documentation, and input validation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Copy-pasted analysis code is the top source of bugs in R. Functions enable testing, reuse, and documentation.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for duplicated code blocks. Check that functions have roxygen2 comments documenting parameters and return values.
baseContent.exampleComment: This data cleaning logic appears in 3 scripts. Extract it into a function in a shared R file.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Extract repeated code into functions. Document with roxygen2.

items.text: Are package dependencies managed properly (renv, explicit library calls)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use renv for reproducible environments. Load packages explicitly with library() at the top. Avoid library() inside functions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without renv, code that works on your machine fails on others due to version mismatches. Hidden package loads cause confusion.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for renv.lock file. Verify all library() calls are at the top of the script. Check for :: usage vs library().
baseContent.exampleComment: There's no renv.lock. Running this on another machine may fail with different package versions. Initialize renv.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use renv for reproducibility. Load packages at the top of scripts.

items.text: Is global state avoided (no modifying the global environment inside functions)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Functions should not use <<- to modify global variables. They should take inputs as parameters and return outputs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Global state makes functions unpredictable. The same function call can produce different results depending on hidden global variables.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for <<- assignment. Check for functions that rely on variables not passed as parameters.
baseContent.exampleComment: This function uses <<- to modify a global counter. Pass it as a parameter and return the updated value instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Avoid global state. Functions should take inputs and return outputs. Never use <<-.

items.text: Are dplyr verbs used correctly (mutate, filter, select, group_by, summarize)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use the right dplyr verb for each operation. Don't use filter() when you mean select(). Use across() for operations on multiple columns.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong verbs produce wrong results silently. Using base R subsetting instead of dplyr is less readable and more error-prone.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that verb chains produce the intended output. Verify group_by() is followed by summarize() or ungroup().
baseContent.exampleComment: This filter() selects columns — use select() instead. filter() is for rows.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use the right verb: filter for rows, select for columns, mutate for new columns, summarize for aggregation.

items.text: Are join operations type-correct and handling missing matches?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use the right join type (left_join, inner_join, anti_join). Check for unexpected row multiplication from many-to-many joins.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong join types silently drop rows (inner) or create NAs (left). Many-to-many joins multiply rows, inflating results.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check row counts before and after joins. Verify the join key is unique in at least one table. Check for unexpected NAs.
baseContent.exampleComment: This left_join increases row count from 1000 to 3500 — the join key isn't unique in the right table. Check for duplicates.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Check row counts after joins. Verify join keys are unique. Handle NAs from unmatched rows.

baseContent.whatItMeans: NA handling should be explicit: use na.rm = TRUE in aggregations, is.na() for checking, tidyr::drop_na() or replace_na() for cleaning.
baseContent.whyItMatters: NAs propagate silently in R. mean(c(1, 2, NA)) returns NA. Unhandled NAs produce NA results throughout the analysis.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check aggregation functions for na.rm parameter. Verify NA handling strategy is documented and consistent.
baseContent.exampleComment: mean(x) returns NA if any value is NA. Add na.rm = TRUE or handle NAs explicitly before aggregating.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Handle NAs explicitly. Document your NA strategy (remove, impute, or propagate).

items.text: Are data types checked after reading data (chr vs num, factors)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Check column types after reading CSV/Excel. Numeric columns may import as character. Dates may need parsing with lubridate.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Type mismatches cause silent errors. Summing a character column returns an error or wrong result. Factor levels affect plots.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check str() or glimpse() output. Verify numeric columns are numeric, dates are Date/POSIXct, and factors have correct levels.
baseContent.exampleComment: The price column imported as character due to a '$' in the CSV. Parse with parse_number() or readr's col_double().
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always check column types after import. Use readr's col_types for explicit type specification.

items.text: Are ggplot layers well-organized and aesthetics mapped correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Map aesthetics in aes() (data-dependent) not outside (fixed values). Layer geoms logically. Use theme_* for consistent styling.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Aesthetics mapped outside aes() don't show legends. Poorly layered plots hide data. Inconsistent themes look unprofessional.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that color/size inside aes() map to data variables. Verify fixed colors are outside aes(). Check theme consistency.
baseContent.exampleComment: color = 'blue' inside aes() creates a legend entry 'blue'. Move it outside aes() for a fixed blue color.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Data-mapped aesthetics go in aes(). Fixed visual properties go outside. Use a consistent theme.

items.text: Do plots have clear titles, axis labels, and legends?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every published plot should have a descriptive title, labeled axes with units, and clear legend entries.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unlabeled plots are ambiguous. Readers can't interpret results without knowing what axes represent and what units are used.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for labs() or ggtitle(). Verify axis labels include units. Check that legend titles are descriptive.
baseContent.exampleComment: This plot has no title and the y-axis says 'value'. Add labs(title = 'Monthly Revenue', y = 'Revenue (USD)').
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Label everything. Titles, axes, and legends should be self-explanatory.

items.text: Are scales and transformations appropriate for the data?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use log scales for exponential data, date scales for time series, and appropriate color scales (viridis for continuous, qualitative for categories).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Linear scales on exponential data hide patterns. Truncated axes exaggerate differences. Poor color choices mislead readers.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that y-axis starts at 0 for bar charts. Verify log scales are labeled. Check color accessibility.
baseContent.exampleComment: This bar chart's y-axis starts at 90, exaggerating a 5% difference to look like 500%. Start at 0.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Choose scales that honestly represent the data. Start bar charts at zero. Use log scales for exponential data.

items.text: Are random seeds set for reproducible results?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use set.seed() before any random operations (sampling, simulations, cross-validation). Document the seed value.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without set.seed(), results change on every run. Other researchers can't reproduce your findings. Tests become flaky.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for random operations (sample, rnorm, train/test splits). Verify set.seed() is called before them.
baseContent.exampleComment: This cross-validation has no set.seed(). Results will differ on every run. Add set.seed(42) before the split.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always set seeds before random operations. Document which seed was used.

items.text: Are file paths relative (no hardcoded absolute paths)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use relative paths from the project root, here::here(), or rprojroot. Never use /Users/yourname/... or setwd().
baseContent.whyItMatters: Absolute paths break on any other machine. setwd() changes global state and breaks when scripts are sourced in different order.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for absolute paths and setwd(). Replace with here::here() or relative paths from the project root.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses setwd('/Users/john/project'). Use here::here('data', 'input.csv') for portable paths.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use here::here() for file paths. Never hardcode absolute paths or use setwd().

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use testthat for unit testing R functions. Test edge cases: empty inputs, NAs, wrong types, boundary values.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untested data processing functions silently produce wrong results. Tests catch regressions when code or data changes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for test files in tests/testthat/. Verify new functions have corresponding tests.
baseContent.exampleComment: This data cleaning function has no tests. Add tests for empty data frames, NAs in key columns, and expected output shape.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test data processing functions with testthat. Cover edge cases like empty data and NAs.

items.text: Are R Markdown documents structured for reproducibility?

baseContent.whatItMeans: R Markdown should load packages in the first chunk, set global options, and run top-to-bottom without manual intervention.
baseContent.whyItMatters: R Markdown that requires running chunks out of order or manual variable setup is not reproducible.
baseContent.howToVerify: Knit the document fresh (clear environment first). Verify it runs top-to-bottom without errors.
baseContent.exampleComment: This R Markdown depends on variables from the global environment. Add all data loading to the document itself.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: R Markdown must run top-to-bottom from a clean environment. Include all data loading.
